title
Business plan for AI agents
ms.reviewer
ssumner
description
Discover how to identity use cases for AI agents that align with organizational goals, enhances operations, and drives innovation.
author
stephen-sumner
ms.author
pnp
ms.date
02/13/2026
ms.update-cycle
180-days
ms.topic
concept-article
ms.collection
ce-skilling-ai-copilot
Business plan for AI agents
Successful AI agent adoption starts with a clear business plan. Every organization should create one before exploring tools or building solutions. This plan sets the direction for where to invest, which problems to solve, and how to measure success. It prevents scattered efforts and helps the organization focus on work that produces strong, repeatable value.
:::image type="content" source="./images/ai-agent-adoption.svg" alt-text="Diagram showing a horizontal workflow with four connected phases: plan for agents (substeps are business plan, technology plan, organizational readiness, and data architecture). Govern and secure agents (substeps are Responsible AI, Governance and Security, and Prepare environment). Build agents (substeps are single and multi-agent systems and process to build agents). Manage agents integrate (subprocesses Integrate agents and operate agents)." lightbox="./images/ai-agent-adoption.svg" border="false":::
Figure 1. Microsoft's AI agent adoption process.
When not to use AI agents
Before you choose to use an AI agent, it helps to know when an agent isn't a good fit. Agents can add extra complexity. Some tasks don't need that complexity. Setting these limits early helps you focus on places where agents truly add value. Ask yourself these questions:
Structured or predictable task? If the steps are clear, repeatable, and follow strict rules, use regular code or nongenerative AI models. These options are faster, cheaper, and more reliable for fixed workflows.
Static knowledge retrieval? If the goal is to answer questions or summarize content from a fixed set of documents, use a classic retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) approach. If the task doesn't need tools or multistep reasoning, an agent is unnecessary.
Examples include FAQ bots, document search with summaries, and simple knowledge assistants. You can build these RAG solutions in Microsoft Foundry .
Use the following decision tree to check whether your use case needs an agent. If you answer "No" to the first two questions, your scenario likely requires the reasoning and tool use that agents provide.
:::image type="complex" source="./images/ai-agent-decision-tree.svg" alt-text="Decision tree diagram for selecting AI agent solutions based on business and technology requirements." lightbox="./images/ai-agent-decision-tree.svg" border="false":::
A decision tree that guides organizations through decisions about when and how to use AI agents. It starts with "Potential agent use case" and branches into multiple decision paths. The business plan path determines if AI agents should be used. If the answer is "No," the path leads to "Use code or nongenerative AI models" with icons for GitHub, Microsoft Fabric, AI models in Foundry, and Machine Learning. If Yes, it asks if the task involves static question or answer or content generation without reasoning. The technology plan path checks if SaaS agents meet functional requirements. If Yes, the path leads to Use SaaS agents. There are icons representing Microsoft 365 Copilot agents (App Builder, Workflows, Researcher, Analyst, Surveys). Then there are icons for GitHub Copilot agent, Microsoft Fabric data agents, Azure Copilot agents, Dynamics 365 agents, and Security Copilot agents. If SaaS agents don't meet needs, the path leads to "Build AI agents" with options for GPUs & Containers on Azure infrastructure, Microsoft Foundry (PaaS pro-code), and Copilot Studio (SaaS no/low-code). You're going to start with multiple-agent systems if the use case crosses security and compliance boundaries, has multiple teams involved, or you know there's going to be future growth of this system. Unless the system is low complexity, all other use cases should start with a single agent test to see if it could meet your requirements. Depending on the result, you'll align with a multi-agent system or single-agent system.
:::image-end:::
Microsoft facilitation:
For nongenerative AI solutions, see Microsoft Fabric data science . See also the prebuilt speech, language, and translator models in Foundry Tools . Build your own predictive models in Azure Machine Learning .
When to use AI agents
After you rule out cases where agents don't help, look for situations where they do create real value. Agents are different from normal software. Instead of following a fixed set of steps, they can reason, plan, and use tools to decide what to do next (see What is an AI agent? ). To get the most benefit, choose business problems where this flexibility matters. Agents are a good fit when:
The task requires multistep decisions. An agent is useful when the system must make choices along the way. Choices include reading information, evaluating it, deciding on the next step, and checking its own work. A support-ticket triage system is a good example. The agent reads the request, checks logs, tries a fix, checks the result, and escalates only when needed. If the workflow changes based on what the system sees or discovers, an agent is a strong fit.
The task uses many tools or systems. Agents perform well when they must call different tools or services in a flexible order. They can choose which API to call, when to call it, and how to combine the results. Think about an expense-processing flow. The agent reads receipts, checks policy rules, calls an approval API, records the decision, and updates finance systems. If the work spans multiple platforms and requires dynamic orchestration, an agent can reduce complexity.
The task needs adaptive behavior. Some tasks don't come with clean inputs. Users might provide incomplete information, unclear requests, or mixed signals. Agents can interpret intent, fill in gaps, and choose the right steps. A customer-service agent is a good example. The agent reads the question, interprets the meaning, checks the knowledge base, finds order information, and creates a personalized reply. If the task needs flexibility and interpretation, an agent is appropriate.
Microsoft facilitation:
See the Microsoft Scenario Library , AI Use Cases catalog , and Sample Solution Gallery to benchmark internal ideas against proven patterns.
How to prioritize AI agent use cases
Not every agent idea delivers the same value. Some ideas are valuable but hard to build. Others are easy to build but have little business impact. A scoring system helps your team compare ideas and choose the ones worth doing first. Use a 1–5 scale across three areas: business impact, technical feasibility, and user desirability. A score of one means low. A score of five means high. This scoring system gives you a clear, side-by-side view of which use cases are strong and which ones need more work.
:::image type="complex" source="./images/prioritize-agent-use-cases.png" alt-text="Diagram that shows a three-part evaluation framework showing business impact, technical feasibility, and user desirability criteria." lightbox="./images/prioritize-agent-use-cases.png" border="false":::
Diagram with three sections. First section shows business impact evaluation criteria: executive strategy alignment, business value, and change management timeframe, each rated from low to high impact. Second section shows technical feasibility evaluation criteria: implementation and operation risks, sufficient safeguards, and technology fit, each rated from low to high feasibility. Third section shows user desirability assessment factors including user pain points, solution acceptance, change readiness, and stakeholder support for AI agent implementations.
:::image-end:::
Evaluate business impact
This section explains how to judge whether a use case matters to the business. Think about whether it supports strategy, creates real value, and fits within a reasonable adoption window.
Executive strategy alignment : Check whether the use case supports top business priorities. A strong use case connects directly to funded goals and has clear leadership support. A weak use case might be interesting but doesn't move the business forward. Best practice: If a use case doesn't support strategy, pause it early.
Business value : Consider how the agent improves results. Strong examples include lower costs, faster workflows, better decisions, or improved customer experiences. Use the four value areas in the following diagram to shape expectations:
:::image type="content" source="./images/ai-agent-opportunities.png" alt-text="Diagram showing four quadrants of AI agent opportunities: Reshape business processes and Enrich employee experiences (internal impact), plus Reinvent customer engagement and Accelerate innovation (external impact). Each quadrant contains specific examples of how AI agents can create business value." lightbox="./images/ai-agent-opportunities.png" border="false":::
Reshape business processes (internal impact). Improve core operations by automating work that normally requires judgment across several systems. For example, an agent can read documents, check policies, update systems, and complete multistep tasks without manual coordination.
Enrich employee experiences (internal impact). Reduce the time employees spend gathering, reviewing, or summarizing information so they can focus on decisions that matter. For example, an agent can collect data from multiple sources and prepare clear summaries that speed up planning and analysis.
Reinvent customer engagement (customer impact). Give customers fast, accurate answers by understanding their needs and responding with context-aware information. For example, an agent can interpret an open-ended question, look up the right details, and reply with a personalized solution.
Accelerate innovation (customer impact). Help teams bring better products and services to customers by quickly analyzing signals and testing early ideas. For example, an agent can scan market inputs, compare options, and highlight insights that guide the next step in development.
Change management timeframe : Estimate how much time and effort the rollout will require. Short, manageable timelines signal strong readiness. Long, disruptive timelines signal a harder path. Best practice: Start with use cases that users can adopt quickly to build momentum.
Measure technical feasibility
This section helps you understand whether your organization can build and operate the agent safely and reliably.
Implementation and operation risks. Identify risks early. Strong candidates have known risks and clear mitigation plans. Weak candidates have unclear risks or no plan to handle them. Best practice: If you can't name the risks, you can't manage them. Pause until the risks are understood.
Sufficient safeguards : Confirm compliance, security, and responsible AI measures. Strong use cases have mature safeguards. Weak ones have unclear governance or potential exposure. Best practice: Never advance a use case with unclear or incomplete safeguards.
Technology fit : Check whether the agent works with existing systems and tools. Strong alignment makes development easier. Poor alignment increases complexity and risk. Best practice: Pick use cases that fit well with current infrastructure and data access patterns.
Validate value through rapid piloting. Before investing heavily, run a small pilot in tools like Microsoft Copilot Studio or Microsoft Foundry to test whether an agent can actually handle the work. Best practice: Pilot the hardest steps. If the agent succeeds there, you can move ahead confidently.
Measure user desirability
This section explains whether people want the solution and are likely to use it. Even high-value, technically feasible use cases can fail without user buy-in.
Key personas : Check whether you understand the users and stakeholders who are affected. Strong candidates have clearly defined personas with well-understood needs. Weak candidates don't.
Value proposition : Consider whether users see clear benefits. Strong use cases solve real pain points and feel meaningful. Weak ones feel optional or unimportant. Best practice: Talk to users early. Validate their interest directly.
Change resistance : Estimate how willing users are to adopt the solution. Strong candidates face little resistance. Weak candidates face hesitation, mistrust, or disruption concerns. Best practice: Choose early use cases with motivated or eager users.
Define success metrics
Clear success metrics help you understand whether an AI agent is doing what the business needs. They also help you make better investment decisions. Without metrics, it's hard to tell if the agent is creating value or simply adding cost. Use the following steps to build simple, measurable, and reliable metrics before any development begins.
Set baseline business goals. Identify the KPIs the agent must improve and measure current performance whenever possible. For existing processes, record today's performance to create a baseline. For new processes or early-stage areas, estimate initial targets and refine them over time as the work matures. Best practice: Keep KPIs simple and choose only the ones that directly show whether the agent helps.
Use business metrics as decision gates. Apply these metrics at each stage of development to guide investment decisions. Use them to decide whether the project should continue, change direction, or stop. If a pilot doesn't meet the agreed-upon results, pause the work and reassess the use case to avoid wasted time and cost. Best practice: Treat decision gates seriously. Make "go" or "no-go" choices based on the data, not assumptions or optimism.
Evaluate post-deployment performance. Continue measuring results after the agent is live. Compare actual performance against the target KPIs and check whether the agent is delivering the expected value. If the agent falls short, use the data to decide whether to refine the design, retire the solution, or shift resources to a better opportunity. Best practice: Review performance on a regular schedule so you can address problems early and track improvements over time.
This structured approach keeps every AI agent initiative accountable to business value and supports continuous improvement across the entire AI portfolio.
Next step
[!div class="nextstepaction"]
Technology plan

Key personas : Check whether you understand the users and stakeholders who are affected. Strong candidates have clearly defined personas with well-understood needs. Weak candidates don't.
Value proposition : Consider whether users see clear benefits. Strong use cases solve real pain points and feel meaningful. Weak ones feel optional or unimportant. Best practice: Talk to users early. Validate their interest directly.
Change resistance : Estimate how willing users are to adopt the solution. Strong candidates face little resistance. Weak candidates face hesitation, mistrust, or disruption concerns. Best practice: Choose early use cases with motivated or eager users.
Define success metrics
Clear success metrics help you understand whether an AI agent is doing what the business needs. They also help you make better investment decisions. Without metrics, it's hard to tell if the agent is creating value or simply adding cost. Use the following steps to build simple, measurable, and reliable metrics before any development begins.
Set baseline business goals. Identify the KPIs the agent must improve and measure current performance whenever possible. For existing processes, record today's performance to create a baseline. For new processes or early-stage areas, estimate initial targets and refine them over time as the work matures. Best practice: Keep KPIs simple and choose only the ones that directly show whether the agent helps.
Use business metrics as decision gates. Apply these metrics at each stage of development to guide investment decisions. Use them to decide whether the project should continue, change direction, or stop. If a pilot doesn't meet the agreed-upon results, pause the work and reassess the use case to avoid wasted time and cost. Best practice: Treat decision gates seriously. Make "go" or "no-go" choices based on the data, not assumptions or optimism.
Evaluate post-deployment performance. Continue measuring results after the agent is live. Compare actual performance against the target KPIs and check whether the agent is delivering the expected value. If the agent falls short, use the data to decide whether to refine the design, retire the solution, or shift resources to a better opportunity. Best practice: Review performance on a regular schedule so you can address problems early and track improvements over time.
This structured approach keeps every AI agent initiative accountable to business value and supports continuous improvement across the entire AI portfolio.
Next step
[!div class="nextstepaction"]
Technology plan

OpenCognit
Build Your Zero Human Company.
The AI agent orchestration OS — CEO orchestrator, persistent memory, real execution, atomic budgets. Self-hosted. No cloud lock-in.
⚠️ License Change Notice
This repository contains OpenCognit Community Edition v0.9.0, the final AGPL-3.0 release.
As of June 2026, OpenCognit is no longer open source. Active development continues under a commercial license as OpenCognit Pro .
The Community Edition remains available under AGPL-3.0 but is no longer maintained . Security patches and new features are exclusive to Pro.
Read the full announcement →
What is OpenCognit?
OpenCognit is an AI agent orchestration OS — not a chatbot, not a single-agent wrapper. It runs a virtual company of autonomous AI agents that work together without you watching.
You set a goal. The CEO agent breaks it down, assigns tasks to specialists, reviews their work with a built-in Critic loop, and escalates blockers — while you sleep.
You → Goal → CEO Agent → Dev Agent → Writer Agent → Researcher Agent
↑ ↓ ↓
Persistent Memory ←── Critic ←── Results ←────┘
OpenCognit Pro vs Community Edition
Feature

From €29/mo
Core Features
CEO Orchestrator — Extended Thinking before delegation
Persistent Memory — MemPalace + Semantic + SOUL documents per agent
Critic Loop — Every output reviewed before "done"
Atomic Budgets — Hard limits enforced at the cent level
Task DAG — Dependencies auto-resolve, downstream tasks unlock
War Room — Live agent grid with real-time execution traces
Org Chart — Agent hierarchies, roles, and peer meetings
Plugin System — Custom adapters, widgets, API endpoints
MCP Server — Use OpenCognit from Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf
CLI Executor — Live bash streaming with SSE
Quick Start (Community Edition)
⚠️ The Community Edition is provided as-is without support or updates.
# 1. Clone
git clone https://github.com/OpenCognit/opencognit.git
cd opencognit
# 2. Setup
bash setup.sh
# 3. Run
npm run dev
The UI opens at http://localhost:3200 . The API runs on http://localhost:3201 .
Get OpenCognit Pro
OpenCognit Pro is the actively developed, commercially licensed version with enterprise security, priority support, and regular updates.

Why the license change?
OpenCognit started as an AGPL project to push the boundaries of autonomous AI agents. After 6 months of development, the reality is clear:
AGPL blocks enterprise adoption — legal teams reject it
No sustainable funding — 30 stars don't pay server bills
Competitors (Paperclip, CrewAI) have 1000× more resources with MIT/Apache
To keep building OpenCognit into a production-grade product , we need a sustainable model. The Community Edition remains free and open (AGPL). Pro funds continued development.

title_original: Which AI Use Cases Are Actually Delivering ROI Today? - LinkedIn
body_original:
Artificial Intelligence is no longer a futuristic add-on; it’s a revenue engine. Nearly 49% of technology leaders in a 2024 - 2025 survey said that AI was “fully integrated” into their company’s core business strategy, according to PwC . Yet even with these many organizations using AI, only a minority have turned these early experiments into production-scale ROI.
So the real question every technology leader is asking is:
“Which AI use cases will generate ROI fast, without requiring a massive overhaul of our tech stack?”
As a custom software development company deeply involved in building AI, SaaS, mobile, and web applications, we evaluate this question daily with CEOs and product owners.
This guide breaks down real-world AI use cases that are genuinely producing measurable returns and how companies like yours can adopt them with minimal risk.
Why This Matters: Are You Investing in AI or Gambling with AI?
Before adopting any AI-powered solution, technology leaders want clarity on:
· Which AI investments directly improve revenue or reduce costs?
· What’s the payback period?
· Which use cases can be embedded into custom web/mobile apps with low complexity?
· How do companies ensure the model remains accurate, compliant, and secure?
Let’s explore the type of AI applications that are delivering proven, measurable ROI across industries, backed by real case examples.
AI applications that Matter
1. AI for Lead Qualification & Sales Enablement (ROI: Improvement in Conversion)
Companies developing AI software for sales experience notice a significant increase in lead conversion rates. The ROI comes from faster lead scoring, automated follow-ups, predictive purchase likelihood, buyer intent classification, and pipeline forecasting.
An apt example of such a platform is LeadGenAI , a lead-generation platform built to generate higher-quality leads and faster qualification cycles, with features like automated outreach sequences and NLP-based message optimization.
Is this right for you?
Ask yourself:
· Do your sales reps waste time on low-intent leads?
· Do you lack insights into which leads convert?
· Do you want AI-driven workflows integrated into your CRM or SaaS product?
If yes, this AI use case pays back extremely fast.
2. AI-Powered Automation of Manual, Repetitive Workflows (ROI: Cost Reduction)
AI can automate back-office tasks. High-ROI automation areas include document processing, data extraction, email classification and responses, report generation, appointment scheduling, and task prioritization.
Case Example: DataMine (AI for Data Extraction & Automation) , an intelligent automation system that extracts structured data from unstructured documents, performs auto-classification, and reduces manual effort significantly.
ROI: Work that took hours now finishes in minutes.
Questions to assess fit:
· Do your teams rely heavily on spreadsheets or manual data input?
· Are there workflows repeated across departments?
· Do you spend significant time reconciling or validating data?
This is usually the easiest and quickest AI project to execute.
3. AI-Powered Predictive Analytics (ROI: Efficiency Boost Across Operations)
Predictive analytics now drives real gains in demand forecasting, preventive maintenance, inventory optimization, and customer churn predictions.
Netflix is one of the most popular examples of predictive analytics in action. The platform analyzes billions of data points like watch history, viewing duration, interaction patterns, time-of-day behavior, and even pause/rewind actions, to predict what each user is most likely to watch next. This prediction-driven personalization is so accurate that over 80% of the content streamed on Netflix comes from its recommendation and prediction engine. The result is lower churn, higher engagement, and better content investment decisions.
Fits best for: Companies with structured data history like ERP logs, CRM activity, operations metrics, etc.
4. AI in Healthcare & Wellness Apps (ROI: Higher User Engagement + Reduced Support Load)
The healthcare industry has become one of the fastest adopters of AI.
Case Example: LifeFit AI , a wellness application that blends AI, computer vision, and integrated health data to deliver truly personalized fitness and nutrition insights. The platform includes AI-powered meal planning, speech-to-text food logging, and OCR/image & barcode recognition, allowing users to log meals simply by speaking, uploading photos, or scanning packaged items. By integrating with the Spoonacular API, the app instantly interprets food items, calculates nutritional value, and updates daily intake.
LifeFit AI also incorporates 3D body scanning (via Fit Xpress 3DLOOK) to track measurements and physical changes over time. It syncs with external fitness trackers and health apps to import steps, workouts, and activity data, enabling adaptive recommendations based on each user’s habits and goals. This combination of AI + APIs + real-time health data makes LifeFit AI a strong example of how intelligent wellness platforms can offer hyper-personalized guidance and improve long-term engagement.
Questions to consider:
· Do you want to increase personalization in your app?
· Can AI reduce dependency on human coaches/therapists?
· Is real-time decision-making important for your product?
Recommended by LinkedIn
Why Your AI Still Sounds Dumb
Kal Prince
1 year ago
Why 95% of Salespeople Will be Replaced by AI Within…
Matthew King
9 years ago
Crafting Powerful AI Prompts: The COSTAR Method for…
Patrick O'Briant
1 year ago
AI in wellness leads to better UX - higher stickiness - better monetization.
5. AI Chatbots & Virtual Assistants (ROI: Lower Customer Support Costs)
Businesses using AI chatbots can save up to 30% on operational expenses. ( IBM ) Use Cases include customer support, appointment management, HR onboarding, internal help-desks, eCommerce shopping assistants, etc.
Case Example: MindMentor AI (AI-driven Coaching Product) , an AI-powered coaching assistant that detects emotional sentiment, guides users with structured cognitive frameworks, and provides actionable insights.
Shopify’s AI assistant Sidekick helps merchants perform tasks like generating product descriptions, analyzing sales data, creating marketing campaigns, and answering store-management questions. It works as a real-time co-pilot for business owners, showing how AI can streamline workflows and reduce operational overhead inside SaaS platforms.
To sum up, AI assistants are not limited to customer support; they can deliver deep, personalized guidance across industries.
6. AI in Recommendation Systems (ROI: Increase in End-User Spend)
Amazon showcases one of the world's most effective recommendation engines, using AI to personalize product suggestions, surface relevant alternatives, and predict what each customer is most likely to buy. Similarly, Spotify leverages AI-driven recommendation models to curate personalized playlists like Discover Weekly and Daily Mix , driving user engagement through hyper-tailored music discovery.
These systems work exceptionally well in eCommerce, EdTech, media platforms, and SaaS products with complex user journeys. By delivering personalized product suggestions, behavior-based nudges, and automated content curation, recommendation engines significantly amplify user engagement and conversion rates.
7. Generative AI for Content, Code, and Design (ROI: Productivity Gains)
Generative AI is reshaping how modern teams create, build, and iterate. Tools like GitHub Copilot help developers write and debug code faster, while platforms such as Canva Magic Studio and Adobe Firefly enable designers to instantly generate layouts, visuals, and creative variations. Even productivity apps like Notion AI assist teams by summarizing content, drafting documents, and automating routine writing tasks.
High-ROI GenAI use cases include auto-generating UI components, producing marketing content, summarizing customer chats and feedback, writing SQL queries, creating reports, and generating QA test cases.
Companies planning custom software should evaluate GenAI-driven internal tools to accelerate app development timelines and boost overall efficiency.
8. AI for Fraud Detection & Document Verification (ROI: Loss Reduction + Faster Approvals)
AI-powered fraud detection and document verification have become standard across industries such as FinTech, Insurance, Logistics, and digital onboarding platforms. Popular services like Stripe Radar use machine learning to identify suspicious transactions in real time, while platforms like Onfido and Jumio automate identity verification by analyzing IDs, facial biometrics, and behavioral patterns. These examples highlight how AI-driven verification can drastically reduce manual review time and eliminate fraudulent activity early in the process.
ROI is generated by instant fraud flagging, automated verification, faster customer onboarding, and reduced manual review time; all of which streamline operations and protect businesses from financial and compliance risks.
9. AI in Personalized Learning & Coaching Platforms (ROI: Higher User Retention + Scalable Expertise)
The demand for personalized, self-paced guidance is exploding, and this is where AI-powered learning and coaching assistants truly shine. MindMentorAI is a strong example: it delivers structured cognitive frameworks, learns user behavior patterns, and adapts guidance dynamically, making it highly suitable for EdTech platforms, employee learning systems, or personal growth products. Built using LangChain, the platform acts as an AI copilot that stays aligned with coaching principles. It supports voice interactions through speech-to-text and text-to-speech, allows users to upload documents for tailored summaries and coaching prompts, and provides real-time refinement controls such as like/dislike and rephrase.
Behind the scenes, MindMentorAI includes a secure admin panel for managing coaching topics, prompt libraries, and subscription-based access. With advanced prompt engineering, safety guardrails, voice integration, and multi-tenant tooling, it demonstrates how AI agents can deliver scalable, personalized coaching experiences, similar to how AI phone agents or virtual tutors operate.
Another real-world example is Duolingo , which uses AI to personalize language-learning paths, predict where a learner may struggle, and adjust lesson difficulty dynamically. Its AI-driven feedback, automated pronunciation evaluation, and personalized practice sessions demonstrate how adaptive learning systems boost engagement and improve learning outcomes at scale.
10. AI-Enhanced Mobile & Web Apps with Smart Features
Modern applications, whether SaaS, mobile, or enterprise, are increasingly expected to be intelligent by default. Leading apps like Google Photos use AI for smart search and automatic tagging, while Gmail enhances productivity with features like Smart Compose and Smart Reply. Similarly, platforms like Notion and Slack now integrate AI-driven suggestions, summarization, and contextual recommendations to streamline user workflows.
Trending smart features include voice commands, intelligent search, auto-tagging, personalized recommendations, and proactive notifications. Adding even a single AI-powered layer to an existing app can dramatically enhance user experience, increase engagement, and improve retention.
How Should You Decide Which AI Use Case to Build First?
Use this quick checklist:
· Do we have a clear, measurable business outcome? (Revenue increase, cost savings, time reduction)
· Do we have the minimum data required? (No need for “big data;” clean, relevant data wins.)
· Can AI integrate into our existing mobile/web/SaaS platform?
· Will end users actually feel the improvement?
· Can we start with a small Proof-of-Concept in 3–6 weeks?
Recommended First Steps for Companies Planning AI Adoption
· Start with an AI Business Discovery Workshop: Clarify key processes that drain time, data currently available, quick-win opportunities, and long-term automation roadmap.
· Build a small POC before a full product: Avoid investing big until you validate model accuracy, user acceptance, and integration complexity.
· Modernize your tech stack only where needed: Most companies can integrate AI without rewriting their entire platform.
· Plan for security & compliance, especially for Healthcare, Finance, Legal, and HR domains.
· Iterate fast as AI improves with usage.
Final Thoughts:
AI ROI Is Real If You Choose the Right Use Cases
AI will not magically fix broken processes. But when applied strategically, especially inside custom SaaS apps , mobile apps, enterprise systems, or automation workflows, it delivers fast, clear, measurable return on investment.
For entrepreneurs and technology leaders, the path is simple: identify a high-impact workflow, start small with a POC, and scale intelligently. With the right approach, AI becomes not a cost center, but a competitive advantage.

body_clean:
Artificial Intelligence is no longer a futuristic add-on; it’s a revenue engine. Nearly 49% of technology leaders in a 2024 - 2025 survey said that AI was “fully integrated” into their company’s core business strategy, according to PwC . Yet even with these many organizations using AI, only a minority have turned these early experiments into production-scale ROI.
So the real question every technology leader is asking is:
“Which AI use cases will generate ROI fast, without requiring a massive overhaul of our tech stack?”
As a custom software development company deeply involved in building AI, SaaS, mobile, and web applications, we evaluate this question daily with CEOs and product owners.
This guide breaks down real-world AI use cases that are genuinely producing measurable returns and how companies like yours can adopt them with minimal risk.
Why This Matters: Are You Investing in AI or Gambling with AI?
Before adopting any AI-powered solution, technology leaders want clarity on:
· Which AI investments directly improve revenue or reduce costs?
· What’s the payback period?
· Which use cases can be embedded into custom web/mobile apps with low complexity?
· How do companies ensure the model remains accurate, compliant, and secure?
Let’s explore the type of AI applications that are delivering proven, measurable ROI across industries, backed by real case examples.
AI applications that Matter
1. AI for Lead Qualification & Sales Enablement (ROI: Improvement in Conversion)
Companies developing AI software for sales experience notice a significant increase in lead conversion rates. The ROI comes from faster lead scoring, automated follow-ups, predictive purchase likelihood, buyer intent classification, and pipeline forecasting.
An apt example of such a platform is LeadGenAI , a lead-generation platform built to generate higher-quality leads and faster qualification cycles, with features like automated outreach sequences and NLP-based message optimization.
Is this right for you?
Ask yourself:
· Do your sales reps waste time on low-intent leads?
· Do you lack insights into which leads convert?
· Do you want AI-driven workflows integrated into your CRM or SaaS product?
If yes, this AI use case pays back extremely fast.
2. AI-Powered Automation of Manual, Repetitive Workflows (ROI: Cost Reduction)
AI can automate back-office tasks. High-ROI automation areas include document processing, data extraction, email classification and responses, report generation, appointment scheduling, and task prioritization.
Case Example: DataMine (AI for Data Extraction & Automation) , an intelligent automation system that extracts structured data from unstructured documents, performs auto-classification, and reduces manual effort significantly.
ROI: Work that took hours now finishes in minutes.
Questions to assess fit:
· Do your teams rely heavily on spreadsheets or manual data input?
· Are there workflows repeated across departments?
· Do you spend significant time reconciling or validating data?
This is usually the easiest and quickest AI project to execute.
3. AI-Powered Predictive Analytics (ROI: Efficiency Boost Across Operations)
Predictive analytics now drives real gains in demand forecasting, preventive maintenance, inventory optimization, and customer churn predictions.
Netflix is one of the most popular examples of predictive analytics in action. The platform analyzes billions of data points like watch history, viewing duration, interaction patterns, time-of-day behavior, and even pause/rewind actions, to predict what each user is most likely to watch next. This prediction-driven personalization is so accurate that over 80% of the content streamed on Netflix comes from its recommendation and prediction engine. The result is lower churn, higher engagement, and better content investment decisions.
Fits best for: Companies with structured data history like ERP logs, CRM activity, operations metrics, etc.
4. AI in Healthcare & Wellness Apps (ROI: Higher User Engagement + Reduced Support Load)
The healthcare industry has become one of the fastest adopters of AI.
Case Example: LifeFit AI , a wellness application that blends AI, computer vision, and integrated health data to deliver truly personalized fitness and nutrition insights. The platform includes AI-powered meal planning, speech-to-text food logging, and OCR/image & barcode recognition, allowing users to log meals simply by speaking, uploading photos, or scanning packaged items. By integrating with the Spoonacular API, the app instantly interprets food items, calculates nutritional value, and updates daily intake.
LifeFit AI also incorporates 3D body scanning (via Fit Xpress 3DLOOK) to track measurements and physical changes over time. It syncs with external fitness trackers and health apps to import steps, workouts, and activity data, enabling adaptive recommendations based on each user’s habits and goals. This combination of AI + APIs + real-time health data makes LifeFit AI a strong example of how intelligent wellness platforms can offer hyper-personalized guidance and improve long-term engagement.
Questions to consider:
· Do you want to increase personalization in your app?
· Can AI reduce dependency on human coaches/therapists?
· Is real-time decision-making important for your product?

AI in wellness leads to better UX - higher stickiness - better monetization.
5. AI Chatbots & Virtual Assistants (ROI: Lower Customer Support Costs)
Businesses using AI chatbots can save up to 30% on operational expenses. ( IBM ) Use Cases include customer support, appointment management, HR onboarding, internal help-desks, eCommerce shopping assistants, etc.
Case Example: MindMentor AI (AI-driven Coaching Product) , an AI-powered coaching assistant that detects emotional sentiment, guides users with structured cognitive frameworks, and provides actionable insights.
Shopify’s AI assistant Sidekick helps merchants perform tasks like generating product descriptions, analyzing sales data, creating marketing campaigns, and answering store-management questions. It works as a real-time co-pilot for business owners, showing how AI can streamline workflows and reduce operational overhead inside SaaS platforms.
To sum up, AI assistants are not limited to customer support; they can deliver deep, personalized guidance across industries.
6. AI in Recommendation Systems (ROI: Increase in End-User Spend)
Amazon showcases one of the world's most effective recommendation engines, using AI to personalize product suggestions, surface relevant alternatives, and predict what each customer is most likely to buy. Similarly, Spotify leverages AI-driven recommendation models to curate personalized playlists like Discover Weekly and Daily Mix , driving user engagement through hyper-tailored music discovery.
These systems work exceptionally well in eCommerce, EdTech, media platforms, and SaaS products with complex user journeys. By delivering personalized product suggestions, behavior-based nudges, and automated content curation, recommendation engines significantly amplify user engagement and conversion rates.
7. Generative AI for Content, Code, and Design (ROI: Productivity Gains)
Generative AI is reshaping how modern teams create, build, and iterate. Tools like GitHub Copilot help developers write and debug code faster, while platforms such as Canva Magic Studio and Adobe Firefly enable designers to instantly generate layouts, visuals, and creative variations. Even productivity apps like Notion AI assist teams by summarizing content, drafting documents, and automating routine writing tasks.
High-ROI GenAI use cases include auto-generating UI components, producing marketing content, summarizing customer chats and feedback, writing SQL queries, creating reports, and generating QA test cases.
Companies planning custom software should evaluate GenAI-driven internal tools to accelerate app development timelines and boost overall efficiency.
8. AI for Fraud Detection & Document Verification (ROI: Loss Reduction + Faster Approvals)
AI-powered fraud detection and document verification have become standard across industries such as FinTech, Insurance, Logistics, and digital onboarding platforms. Popular services like Stripe Radar use machine learning to identify suspicious transactions in real time, while platforms like Onfido and Jumio automate identity verification by analyzing IDs, facial biometrics, and behavioral patterns. These examples highlight how AI-driven verification can drastically reduce manual review time and eliminate fraudulent activity early in the process.
ROI is generated by instant fraud flagging, automated verification, faster customer onboarding, and reduced manual review time; all of which streamline operations and protect businesses from financial and compliance risks.
9. AI in Personalized Learning & Coaching Platforms (ROI: Higher User Retention + Scalable Expertise)
The demand for personalized, self-paced guidance is exploding, and this is where AI-powered learning and coaching assistants truly shine. MindMentorAI is a strong example: it delivers structured cognitive frameworks, learns user behavior patterns, and adapts guidance dynamically, making it highly suitable for EdTech platforms, employee learning systems, or personal growth products. Built using LangChain, the platform acts as an AI copilot that stays aligned with coaching principles. It supports voice interactions through speech-to-text and text-to-speech, allows users to upload documents for tailored summaries and coaching prompts, and provides real-time refinement controls such as like/dislike and rephrase.
Behind the scenes, MindMentorAI includes a secure admin panel for managing coaching topics, prompt libraries, and subscription-based access. With advanced prompt engineering, safety guardrails, voice integration, and multi-tenant tooling, it demonstrates how AI agents can deliver scalable, personalized coaching experiences, similar to how AI phone agents or virtual tutors operate.
Another real-world example is Duolingo , which uses AI to personalize language-learning paths, predict where a learner may struggle, and adjust lesson difficulty dynamically. Its AI-driven feedback, automated pronunciation evaluation, and personalized practice sessions demonstrate how adaptive learning systems boost engagement and improve learning outcomes at scale.
10. AI-Enhanced Mobile & Web Apps with Smart Features
Modern applications, whether SaaS, mobile, or enterprise, are increasingly expected to be intelligent by default. Leading apps like Google Photos use AI for smart search and automatic tagging, while Gmail enhances productivity with features like Smart Compose and Smart Reply. Similarly, platforms like Notion and Slack now integrate AI-driven suggestions, summarization, and contextual recommendations to streamline user workflows.
Trending smart features include voice commands, intelligent search, auto-tagging, personalized recommendations, and proactive notifications. Adding even a single AI-powered layer to an existing app can dramatically enhance user experience, increase engagement, and improve retention.
How Should You Decide Which AI Use Case to Build First?
Use this quick checklist:
· Do we have a clear, measurable business outcome? (Revenue increase, cost savings, time reduction)
· Do we have the minimum data required? (No need for “big data;” clean, relevant data wins.)
· Can AI integrate into our existing mobile/web/SaaS platform?
· Will end users actually feel the improvement?
· Can we start with a small Proof-of-Concept in 3–6 weeks?
Recommended First Steps for Companies Planning AI Adoption
· Start with an AI Business Discovery Workshop: Clarify key processes that drain time, data currently available, quick-win opportunities, and long-term automation roadmap.
· Build a small POC before a full product: Avoid investing big until you validate model accuracy, user acceptance, and integration complexity.
· Modernize your tech stack only where needed: Most companies can integrate AI without rewriting their entire platform.
· Plan for security & compliance, especially for Healthcare, Finance, Legal, and HR domains.
· Iterate fast as AI improves with usage.
Final Thoughts:
AI ROI Is Real If You Choose the Right Use Cases
AI will not magically fix broken processes. But when applied strategically, especially inside custom SaaS apps , mobile apps, enterprise systems, or automation workflows, it delivers fast, clear, measurable return on investment.
For entrepreneurs and technology leaders, the path is simple: identify a high-impact workflow, start small with a POC, and scale intelligently. With the right approach, AI becomes not a cost center, but a competitive advantage.

title_original: Onpilot
body_original:
New Regulated and complex operations into AI agents
AI Workforce customized for your business needs
Onpilot connects to your ERP, CRM, support tools, documents, and APIs to automate real business workflows with approvals, permissions, and audit logs built in.
Get $50 in AI credit when you join
Trusted by
Features
Everything enterprises need to deploy AI agents in production.
Agentic Workflows
Turn time-consuming business processes into working AI agents that can search, reason, take action, and escalate.
Deploy Anywhere
Multi-tenant, VPC, on-premise
Security & Governance
Manage permissions, monitor agent activity, control feature access, and keep audit logs for every action.

Your AI Partner
Onpilot customizes around your complex workflows and becomes a true AI partner, not just another tool. Dedicated engineers, tailored agents, real outcomes.
You
Our shop floor uses three ERPs and a custom MES, most AI tools can't model that. Can your agent actually handle our work-order routing?
Onpilot Team
Yes, we'll map your work-order flow end-to-end, wire the agent into SAP, NetSuite, and your MES, and tune it on real historical jobs before go-live.
You
Quality team also wants the agent to escalate defect clusters automatically, but with their own approval chain.
Onpilot Team
We'll model that as a separate sub-agent with your QA policy and Plant Mgr approvals. Your workflows, your guardrails, we build around them.
Integrations
Connect AI agents to the systems your business already runs on.
/ Open a maintenance work order in Jira for the Line 3 conveyor jam
/ Notify the night shift supervisor on Microsoft Teams about Line 2 downtime
/ Post today's quality inspection report to #plant-ops

Solutions & Outcomes
From process to AI agent in days, not months.
Deploy secure AI agents that understand your business data, connect to your systems, and complete real operational work.
Workforce Manufacturing Operations Sales & CRM Support Finance Compliance
Roll out an Onpilot agent for HR and IT support across factory and frontline workers. Employees ask routine questions over chat — software access, PTO policy, shift swaps — and get answers in seconds without opening a ticket.
Example prompt Provision Figma and Notion access for Jane in marketing, and notify the team in Slack .
Apps
Workflows used
Software provisioning
PTO policy lookup
Shift coverage requests
Cut help-desk tickets with an HR + IT agent for frontline teams
Security & Access Control
Your data stays yours.
Secure OAuth connections. Encrypted data. Isolated per team. Never used to train models.
Data isolation
Contact records, deal data, and pipeline information stay scoped per team, no cross-contamination.
OAuth-based access
Connect HubSpot, Salesforce, and Pipedrive through OAuth, we never store your credentials.
No model training on your data
Your data is never used to train AI models. Every query is processed and forgotten.
Granular access control
Control which fields, records, and actions each team member and AI agent can access.
Compliance-ready foundation
Built to support enterprise security expectations, including SOC 2 and GDPR readiness workflows.
Audit logs for every action
Every query, approval, and AI action is logged with full context, so you can review what happened, when, and by whom.
How It Works
AI agents that connect, act, and stay under control
Designed for enterprise, operations, and product teams that need AI agents to work safely inside real systems.
01 Connect
Bring in APIs, databases, documents, CRMs, ERPs, knowledge bases, support tools, and workflow systems.
APIs Databases Documents CRMs ERPs Knowledge bases Support tools Workflows
3,200+
02 Configure
Define what each agent can answer, what tools it can use, which actions need approval, and who can access it.
03 Deploy
Launch agents inside your website, SaaS product, internal dashboard, Slack, Teams, email, or customer portal.
04 Control
Monitor usage, review decisions, manage permissions, and keep a full audit trail of every interaction and action.
100+ teams shaped their AI strategy this quarter
Get your free AI strategy session
See workflows we've already built for teams in your industry. In 30 minutes, we'll walk through Onpilot live and leave you with a tailored AI plan you can take to your team.
Book a demo No commitments

title_original: Google AI Studio’s Interactions API for Gemini models and agents
title_zh: Google AI Studio 推出 Gemini 模型与智能体交互 API
body_original:
Interactions API: our primary interface for Gemini models and agents
Share
x.com
Facebook
LinkedIn
Mail
Copy link
A single unified endpoint for Gemini models and agents with server-side state, background execution, tool combination and multimodal generation.
Ali Çevik
Group Product Manager, Google DeepMind
Philipp Schmid
Developer Relations Engineer, Google DeepMind
Share
x.com
Facebook
LinkedIn
Mail
Copy link
Your browser does not support the audio element.
Listen to article
This content is generated by Google AI. Generative AI is experimental
[[duration]] minutes
Voice
Speed
Voice
Speed
0.75X
1X
1.5X
2X
Today we're announcing that the Interactions API has reached general availability and is now our primary API for interacting with Gemini models and agents. We launched its public beta in December 2025, and it has quickly become developers’ favorite way to build applications with Gemini.
With this GA release, the API now has a stable schema and we also added major new capabilities that developers asked for, including Managed Agents, background execution, Gemini Omni (soon) and more. All of our documentation now defaults to Interactions API and we are working with ecosystem partners to make it the default interface across 3P SDKs and Libraries.
The simplest way to build with Gemini
Whether you're calling a model or running an agent, the Interactions API gets you there in a few lines of code. Pass a model ID for inference, an agent ID for autonomous tasks, set background=True for anything long-running.
Key updates since December
Managed Agents : A single API call provisions a remote Linux sandbox where an agent can reason, execute code, browse the web and manage files. The Antigravity agent ships as the default, and you can define your own custom agents with instructions, skills and data sources.
Background execution : Set background=True on any call. The server runs the interaction asynchronously.
Tool improvements : Mix built-in tools , such as Google Search , Google Maps with your own functions in one request. Tool results can now return images alongside text .
Deep Research upgrades: Two new agent versions (speed vs. depth), collaborative planning, native charts and infographics, and multimodal grounding with images, PDFs and audio.
Media generation: Image generation with Nano Banana 2 and Google Image Search grounding, music with Lyria 3, and expressive speech with multi-speaker TTS.
From Roles to Steps : Simplified schema where every action (user_input, thought, function_call, model_output, etc.) is its own typed step, replacing the old role structure.
Cost and developer optimizations: Flex and Priority tiers let you optimize for cost or latency (Flex offers 50% cost reduction). Errors now pinpoint the exact field. Past interactions are retrievable with 55-day retention on the paid tier.
The new standard for development
The Interactions API is now the default for Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and all our documentation , which includes a toggle to switch snippets back to the legacy format. We recommend using the Interactions API for all new projects and applications.
While the legacy generateContent API remains fully supported and will continue to receive new mainline Gemini models for the foreseeable future, we expect frontier capabilities for long-running models and agents to increasingly land exclusively on the Interactions API. This is because it is designed from the ground up for stateful, agentic workflows. We have published a migration guide to help you transition at your own pace.
An agent-first ecosystem
Most developers are now using coding agents (such as Antigravity) to build applications. To make it easier for agents to stay up to date with the latest API patterns, we built the gemini-interactions-api Skill . It injects best-practice patterns for Interactions API development into your agent's context (streaming, function calling, structured output, Deep Research and more).
Get started
The Interactions API is available through the Python and JavaScript SDKs. If you're already building with one of our supported partners, LiteLLM , Eigent or Agno , you can start using their Interactions API integrations today.
Grab your API key from Google AI Studio and follow the Interactions API documentation to get started. If you’re migrating from generateContent, our migration guide maps every field to the new schema. You can also view the full API Reference .
The Interactions API was built based on developer feedback, and that focus won't change with general availability. Tell us what you need on the developer forum .

Today we're announcing that the Interactions API has reached general availability and is now our primary API for interacting with Gemini models and agents. We launched its public beta in December 2025, and it has quickly become developers’ favorite way to build applications with Gemini.
With this GA release, the API now has a stable schema and we also added major new capabilities that developers asked for, including Managed Agents, background execution, Gemini Omni (soon) and more. All of our documentation now defaults to Interactions API and we are working with ecosystem partners to make it the default interface across 3P SDKs and Libraries.
The simplest way to build with Gemini
Whether you're calling a model or running an agent, the Interactions API gets you there in a few lines of code. Pass a model ID for inference, an agent ID for autonomous tasks, set background=True for anything long-running.
Key updates since December
Managed Agents : A single API call provisions a remote Linux sandbox where an agent can reason, execute code, browse the web and manage files. The Antigravity agent ships as the default, and you can define your own custom agents with instructions, skills and data sources.
Background execution : Set background=True on any call. The server runs the interaction asynchronously.
Tool improvements : Mix built-in tools , such as Google Search , Google Maps with your own functions in one request. Tool results can now return images alongside text .
Deep Research upgrades: Two new agent versions (speed vs. depth), collaborative planning, native charts and infographics, and multimodal grounding with images, PDFs and audio.
Media generation: Image generation with Nano Banana 2 and Google Image Search grounding, music with Lyria 3, and expressive speech with multi-speaker TTS.
From Roles to Steps : Simplified schema where every action (user_input, thought, function_call, model_output, etc.) is its own typed step, replacing the old role structure.
Cost and developer optimizations: Flex and Priority tiers let you optimize for cost or latency (Flex offers 50% cost reduction). Errors now pinpoint the exact field. Past interactions are retrievable with 55-day retention on the paid tier.
The new standard for development
The Interactions API is now the default for Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and all our documentation , which includes a toggle to switch snippets back to the legacy format. We recommend using the Interactions API for all new projects and applications.
While the legacy generateContent API remains fully supported and will continue to receive new mainline Gemini models for the foreseeable future, we expect frontier capabilities for long-running models and agents to increasingly land exclusively on the Interactions API. This is because it is designed from the ground up for stateful, agentic workflows. We have published a migration guide to help you transition at your own pace.
An agent-first ecosystem
Most developers are now using coding agents (such as Antigravity) to build applications. To make it easier for agents to stay up to date with the latest API patterns, we built the gemini-interactions-api Skill . It injects best-practice patterns for Interactions API development into your agent's context (streaming, function calling, structured output, Deep Research and more).
Get started
The Interactions API is available through the Python and JavaScript SDKs. If you're already building with one of our supported partners, LiteLLM , Eigent or Agno , you can start using their Interactions API integrations today.
Grab your API key from Google AI Studio and follow the Interactions API documentation to get started. If you’re migrating from generateContent, our migration guide maps every field to the new schema. You can also view the full API Reference .
The Interactions API was built based on developer feedback, and that focus won't change with general availability. Tell us what you need on the developer forum .

title_original: Enterprise AI agents are doing real work now. They authenticate ...
title_zh: 企业AI智能体已开始执行实际工作。它们进行身份验证...
body_original:
Enterprise AI agents are doing real work now. 💪
They authenticate customers, follow policies, complete workflows, update systems, and hand off complex issues to frontline teams. So the question is no longer, “Can the agent hold a conversation?” It is, “How do we know it is ready for production, and how do we keep improving it once it is live?”
That is where Proactive Quality and Agent Harness come together.
Proactive Quality turns real production conversations into reusable simulations and evaluations. Agent Harness gives teams the safe workspaces, versioning, governance, and rollout controls to act on what they learn. Together, they create a continuous loop:
Test the agent. Measure performance. Improve the workflow. Govern the release. Learn from production. Repeat.
That is how enterprises move faster without losing control. Every conversation becomes a learning opportunity. Every release becomes measurable. Every improvement is backed by evidence.
Read more about our Proactive Quality here 👉️ https://www.observe.ai/blog/introducing-proactive-quality-for-ai-agents?utm_source=organic-social&utm_medium=facebook&utm_campaign=Agent-Harness-Blog | Observe.AI

boardroom

boardroom

Based on a new open-source index from the AI-Driven Enterprise Institute, Nvidia is setting the pace for corporate AI adoption.⁠
The study ranked S&P 500 companies by how aggressively they are adopting AI, using public data like earnings call transcripts, job openings, and patent applications. Nvidia, Amazon, Meta, and SLB all earned perfect company scores, with Walmart, AES, NextEra Energy, Ecolab, Digital Realty, and Chevron rounding out the top 10.⁠
The index does not measure whether AI is already driving financial returns, but it gives companies a clearer way to compare how seriously they are building around the technology. AIDE CEO Paul Cheek said the goal is to give boards and CEOs real data when asking how they stack up against their peers.⁠
(via @cnbc )

title_original: AI and Liability
body_original:
AI and Liability
Simon Willison’s Weblog
Subscribe
Sponsored by: Depot — AI agents write code in seconds. CI shouldn't make them wait minutes. Try Depot CI
25th June 2026 - Link Blog
AI and Liability . Bruce Schneier on the recent German ruling that Google be held liable for errors introduced in their AI overviews:
AI agents are agents of the person or organization that deploys them—and should be treated by the law as such. If a company hired human writers to write its summaries, that company would be liable for inaccuracies in those summaries. [...]
To allow businesses to hide behind the excuse of faulty AI in those same circumstances would be a massive handout to companies, and would introduce disastrous incentives for corporate misbehavior. Why hire human writers, lawyers or doctors when AIs are not only cheaper, but also absolve employers whenever they make a mistake?
Posted 25th June 2026 at 10:28 pm

title_original: Are AI startups building for long term value or just short ... - Facebook
title_zh: AI 初创公司是在构建长期价值还是仅仅追求短期……
body_original:
GHJ's Post
GHJ
June 12 at 9:11 AM ·
Are AI startups building for long term value or just short term hype?
As capital continues flowing into AI companies at record levels, founders, investors and finance leaders are facing growing pressure to prove sustainable growth, defend valuations and prepare for increasingly complex exit environments.
GHJ Corporate Tax Practice Leader Nisha Golcha and Corporate Tax Practice Expert Miso Moon explore why valuation strategy, transaction readiness and tax planning can no longer operate in silos for AI startups preparing for future growth or exit opportunities.
From evolving investor expectations to operational readiness and long term tax strategy, the article highlights why the most successful AI companies are thinking beyond innovation alone.
Read the full article for additional insights into the future of AI startup growth and exit planning.

title_original: How the Forward Deployed Engineer role transformed my ... - LinkedIn
body_original:
I came across the concept of “Forward Deployed Engineer” for the first time during a conversation with one of our Co-Founders.
I came across the concept of “Forward Deployed Engineer” for the first time during a conversation with one of our Co-Founders. That conversation shifted how I think about my own role. Until then, I only knew myself as an Associate Solution Architect - someone who sits between product, engineering, and customers in an Applied AI company.
But whenever I tried explaining my work to friends, I often got:
“Wait… so you gather requirements?”
“Do you just fill spreadsheets?”
“Isn’t that just documentation?”
I used to laugh it off. But deep down I knew the work was so much more. I just didn’t have the language for it. Then I started learning about the Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) role. And everything clicked.
FDEs are engineers who embed with customers to make AI actually work in their real environment. They translate messy workflows into functioning AI systems, iterate with users, solve problems in the wild, and ensure the product creates real business value.
And I realized… that’s exactly what we do as Solution Architects in Phenom.
We:
✨ dig into customer workflows
✨ map their pain points
✨ design AI-enabled solutions
✨ work hands-on with product and engineering
✨ drive adoption and user success
✨ and make sure the customer actually sees value
Most importantly:
We make customers stay.
Not because of contracts.
Not because of demos.
But because the solution works for them in the real world reliably, meaningfully, and every day.
Understanding the FDE role didn’t change my job. It changed how I see my job.
It gave structure to the hybrid, sometimes messy, always impactful nature of what I do.
And honestly, it made me appreciate this role even more.
We’re not “filling spreadsheets.”
We’re the bridge between AI technology and the people who depend on it.
We’re the reason the platform feels intuitive, solves real problems, and becomes part of the customer’s daily workflow.
And whether you call it Solution Architect, FDE, Applied AI Engineer, or anything else it’s the impact that matters.
#AI #AppliedAI #ForwardDeployedEngineer #SolutionArchitecture #CustomerSuccess #ProductEngineering #AICareers
I came across the concept of “Forward Deployed Engineer” for the first time during a conversation with one of our Co-Founders.
I came across the concept of “Forward Deployed Engineer” for the first time during a conversation with one of our Co-Founders. That conversation shifted how I think about my own role. Until then, I only knew myself as an Associate Solution Architect - someone who sits between product, engineering, and customers in an Applied AI company.
But whenever I tried explaining my work to friends, I often got:
“Wait… so you gather requirements?”
“Do you just fill spreadsheets?”
“Isn’t that just documentation?”
I used to laugh it off. But deep down I knew the work was so much more. I just didn’t have the language for it. Then I started learning about the Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) role. And everything clicked.
FDEs are engineers who embed with customers to make AI actually work in their real environment. They translate messy workflows into functioning AI systems, iterate with users, solve problems in the wild, and ensure the product creates real business value.
And I realized… that’s exactly what we do as Solution Architects in Phenom.
We:
✨ dig into customer workflows
✨ map their pain points
✨ design AI-enabled solutions
✨ work hands-on with product and engineering
✨ drive adoption and user success
✨ and make sure the customer actually sees value
Most importantly:
We make customers stay.
Not because of contracts.
Not because of demos.
But because the solution works for them in the real world reliably, meaningfully, and every day.
Understanding the FDE role didn’t change my job. It changed how I see my job.
It gave structure to the hybrid, sometimes messy, always impactful nature of what I do.
And honestly, it made me appreciate this role even more.
We’re not “filling spreadsheets.”
We’re the bridge between AI technology and the people who depend on it.
We’re the reason the platform feels intuitive, solves real problems, and becomes part of the customer’s daily workflow.
And whether you call it Solution Architect, FDE, Applied AI Engineer, or anything else it’s the impact that matters.
#AI #AppliedAI #ForwardDeployedEngineer #SolutionArchitecture #CustomerSuccess #ProductEngineering #AICareers

title_original: From Cloud Migration to AI-Powered Innovation - Paid Program - WSJ
body_original:
Quick Summary
read more
Organizations often treat cloud migration as a logistical infrastructure task rather than a strategic opportunity to modernize workflows and eliminate on-premises constraints.
Adopting cloud-native architectures enables AI to deliver real-time insights, automate decisions, and help teams act faster at scale.
Through a strategic partnership with AWS, Atlassian enables enterprises to centralize fragmented systems and automate complex workflows to reduce costs and accelerate decision-making.
An artificial-intelligence tool created this summary, which was based on the text of the article and checked by a Custom Content from WSJ editor.
Bill Hustad, Head of GTM Ecosystems, Atlassian
Chris Casey, Managing Director ANZ, AWS
Enterprise buyers now evaluate technology through an AI-first lens. Cloud is no longer just infrastructure. It’s the prerequisite for delivering the intelligent, automated experiences they expect. The partnerships that win will be those that combine platform depth with AI-native integrations that drive productivity from day one.
The 15-year alliance between Atlassian and AWS was built on moving businesses to the cloud. Now, with AI reshaping enterprise operations, that migration isn’t optional. It’s existential. Bill Hustad, Atlassian’s head of GTM ecosystems, and Chris Casey, AWS’s managing director ANZ, explain what’s changed.
Q: Cloud migration can feel like a massive undertaking for the enterprise. What’s the case for why it’s worth it?
Bill Hustad: For me, migration implies a lift-and-shift move of what you have today into the cloud. But what we’re doing is a transformation. We’re giving customers the opportunity to fundamentally reimagine how they work with AI-powered collaboration, deeper integrations, and capabilities that simply can’t exist on-prem.
We surveyed 500 executives in our customer base, and the signal is clear: Organizations are seeing 55% greater scalability in the cloud, plus measurable gains in reliability, agility, and cost efficiency. But what’s really accelerating the conversation is AI. More than half of these executives say AI is now a leading reason to migrate.
Cloud also mitigates infrastructure constraints. Just imagine: As we see with AI models and how they scale, having that kind of infinite capacity versus having to procure, build, configure, and manage internally allows companies to focus on what they do best.
We saw this with Delivery Hero, a food-delivery company based in Germany. We worked with them closely alongside AWS, and after moving to cloud, we centralized 40,000 employees on a single system of work. That resulted in a 69% cost reduction in the first three years, and over 1,000 annual maintenance hours redirected to higher-value work.
Q: Why is cloud migration the right starting point when it comes to making the most of AI?
Chris Casey: Cloud migration is the foundation for AI adoption because the technology requires three things that on-premises infrastructure struggles to provide: massive computational power, access to the latest AI models, and the ability to scale dynamically. On-premises, you’re still building infrastructure. In AI, speed matters. And the scale of this shift is still very early, as 85% of global IT spend is still on-premises. The organizations that move now will have an AI-ready foundation. To put it into perspective, we’re seeing AI moving up to 10 times faster than electricity did when it reorganized every factory, home, and industry.
A concrete example of our AI story coming together for customers is the integration of Rovo, Atlassian’s generative AI, with Amazon Quick, our business and intelligence automation platform. When knowledge is available right where people work, they make decisions faster and collaborate more easily. It improves what we’d call “decision-making velocity.”
Q: After an organization migrates, what does that transformation actually look like for teams on a day-to-day basis?
Hustad: The big shift is moving from reactive models to proactive ones. Real-time insights give teams continuous visibility: live dashboards showing project status, velocity, blockers, and dependencies across the entire organization. But more than that, you can start to look into the future. You can see bottlenecks forming across different projects before they become critical.
The other side is risk identification: automatically surfacing things like a missed deadline that will have follow-on effects to other projects, or resource constraints being managed separately from the project itself. Tying those things together gives executives much better visibility to make decisions before they become critical issues.
Ultimately, there are fewer assumptions, more facts, and better visibility into where you’re going and why. That’s what allows teams to plan more effectively and deliver much faster.
Q: With both AWS and Atlassian making significant investments in AI, how is this partnership specifically accelerating AI adoption and delivering tangible value for enterprise customers?
Hustad: A major problem we see holding up AI adoption is something we call the “coordination tax.” Many organizations will have deployed AI tools and seen subsequent productivity gains on a person-by-person basis. But a lot of the time, that individual uplift never translates to business-wide ROI.
It’s a structural problem; not a lack of effort, organization, or vision. It stems from AI being deployed on the wrong infrastructure or a disconnected stack, which forces workers to spend more time on coordination than execution. Hence, “the coordination tax.”
Our partnership creates tangible value by eliminating this “tax.” Atlassian’s Rovo AI is powered by the Teamwork Graph, our unified data intelligence layer that connects across an organization’s entire toolset. When run on AWS’s secure and scalable enterprise-grade infrastructure, organizations can utilize agentic, connected intelligence that helps them do more and collaborate with less friction.
That means less time spent on coordination, searching, aligning, and context switching, and more time on the high-impact work that drives business outcomes. From software delivery to IT service management and strategic planning, Atlassian and AWS are making AI-native teamwork the new standard for the enterprise.
Casey: From our perspective, the growth and breadth of innovation we are seeing from AWS customers is unprecedented. We have the trust of millions of active customers, including the most security-sensitive organizations, as AWS delivers the functionality, security, and operational performance enterprises need. That’s the foundation Atlassian leverages when it builds AI apps with Amazon Bedrock: Enterprise customers get the security and scale they need without compromise.
Q: Looking ahead, how will AWS and Atlassian’s partnership evolve to handle the increasing complexity of software development and service management?
Casey: The future centers on three areas. First, AI-native workflows: We’re moving beyond AI as a feature to AI as the foundation, with intelligent agents that can automate complex workflows, predict project outcomes, and provide recommendations across the entire development lifecycle.
Second, deeper platform integration, making it seamless to move from planning to deployment to operations without context switching. And third, enterprise-scale automation: As software development grows more complex, we’re investing in automation that can handle multiteam coordination, dependency management, and risk mitigation across organizations of different sizes and operating in diverse industries.
Our goal is to ensure that as complexity increases, the tools teams use become simpler, more intelligent, and more powerful. That’s why we’re investing $200 billion in infrastructure in 2026, which is already backed by real customer commitments. The investment also ensures our partners like Atlassian can build on a strong foundation that scales ahead of demand.
Start your AI transformation today with AWS and Atlassian.

title_original: AI Adoption: Subsidized Now, $20/hour Later - LinkedIn
body_original:
AI Adoption: Subsidized Now, $20/hour Later
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Andrew Brown
3mo
Edited
Report this post
Right now will be the cheapest time to adopt AI skills because the tokens are being subsidized via subscriptions. Once we're all learned and we've trained their models to do most tasks, I would expect we will be paying $20 per hour because why wouldn't they charge that?
How do startups work? Get people in the door at all costs,
Kill the competition (skilled people who can do it by hand).
Jack up the price.
If it was doing the job of 3 employees why wouldn't the charge whatever that was before, because you were paying for it.

The thing that keeps them jacking up the prices right now is precisely the competition. If ChatGPT raised prices 10 times, everyone would move to Gemini, Claude, etc. But as AI tools mature and become better and better at their niche, the writing is on the wall. For example, plenty of developers are becoming extremely dependent on Claude Code, since it's the best tool for devs. If Claude Code continues with that edge for a couple more months, they will be able to get away with a price spike.

I keep asking myself this question and what comes back is Socratic method, staying curious, scientific thinking, the 5 whys, first principles thinking etc
If feels like in a world where AI can do most things, and will be able to do more, what we ask it to do and how we evaluate it and how we understand and think about it seems the new necessity.
Would love to hear your thoughts 💭

Muhammad Abdullah
2mo
Report this post
90% of AI startups will be dead by 2028.
Not because AI doesn't work.
Because they never asked users what problem to solve.
The playbook right now:
1. Wrap Claude Opus in a nice UI
2. Raise $5M on vibes
3. Ship features nobody requested
4. Wonder why churn is 20%/month
I watched this exact movie in 2022 with no-code.
2018 with crypto.
2016 with chatbots.
Same plot. Different costume.
The startups that survive?
Superhuman didn't win because of AI.
They won because Rahul called 1,000 users personally.
Linear didn't win because of features.
They won because they knew exactly what devs hated about Jira.
Notion didn't win because of blocks.
They won because they understood how people actually work.
AI is a tool.
Understanding users is the weapon.
Every startup has access to GPT-5.
Not every startup has access to truth.
The ones who talk to users will eat the ones who just ship features.
Same as always.
What's your user feedback system?
And be honest, when did you last call one?
#AI #SaaS #StartupReality

Two AI coding startups just pulled valuations that make you stop scrolling. Cursor's in talks to raise $2B at $50B - forecasting $6B ARR by end of year. Factory went unicorn at $1.5B with $150M fresh capital for enterprise agents.
Same pattern: both shifted hard into enterprise. Cursor was burning cash on consumer per-seat plans until recently. Factory's edge is model switching - Claude, DeepSeek, whatever fits the job.
The days of VC-subsidised all-you-can-eat AI tools are done. The money's following actual revenue now, not hype. $6B run rate four years after founding? That's shocking if you ask me.

LinkedIn
